blocks.content: Cassandra was in bed when the message came. Not asleep — she'd been lying in the dark with the day spread across her, the way good days sometimes needed to be absorbed without light.
Her phone lit up on the nightstand. It was Maya.
Cass. Are you awake?
She looked at the message for a moment before answering.
Yes.
Can we talk? Not a call. Just — this is okay.
Okay.
A long pause. Long enough that Cassandra could picture Maya on the other end, typing and deleting the way she did when a thing was hard to get right.
I talked to Dad tonight. About you. About things.
I know. He said you might reach out.
He told you that?
He said sometimes you need to talk through the door before you can open it.
Another pause.

I don't know how to say this right. I've been trying to figure out the right words for weeks and I think the problem is there aren't any. So I'm just going to say it even though it's going to come out messy.
Say it messy. I'll understand.

I'm scared, Cass. That's the whole of it. I'm scared all the time. Every single day. And when I see you happy — really happy, not the kind you perform — I get more scared, not less. Because happy means invested. Invested means there's more to lose. And I just — I can't breathe sometimes when I think about it.
Maya.

I'm sorry. I told myself I wasn't going to do this. I don't know how to watch you build something knowing what I know. I don't know how to be happy for something that's going to cost you. I know that's not fair. I know you didn't ask me to guard you from your own life. I just — it's not a switch, Cass. It's not something I can just decide to feel differently about.
I'm not asking you to turn it off. I'm asking you to carry it somewhere I can't see it all the time.
I know. I know. I'm trying. 😭
Cassandra sat up in bed. She pressed her back against the headboard and held the phone with both hands. Her chest was doing something she didn't want to name.
Another pause. Longer.
He seems like a good person. From what I've heard.
He is. He really is, Maya.
Does he know? About any of it?
Not yet.
Are you going to tell him?
Yes. When it's the right time.
How do you know when that is? How are you so calm about this? How are you always so calm about this? It's not fair that you're the calm one. You shouldn't have to be the calm one. 😭
Cassandra closed her eyes for a moment. When she opened them the room was the same — dark, quiet, the town outside settled into the specific silence of late night. She typed slowly.

I'm not always calm. I have nights where I'm not calm at all. But I've decided that I'm not going to spend the time I have being afraid of how much time I have. I made that decision a long time ago and I have to keep making it. Every day.
You don't have to make that same decision. You're allowed to be scared. I just need you to love me from inside the fear instead of in front of it.
A very long pause. The kind that meant Maya was sitting somewhere with the phone held against her chest, trying to get herself together.
I don't like this. I want to be okay with it and I'm not. Not fully. Maybe not for a while.
I know.
But I see you, Cass. I see you happy and I see you glowing and I see you with paint in your hair and I — 😭 I'm not going to stand in the way of it. I promise I'm not.
Cassandra read that message twice. Then a third time. Something moved through her chest that wasn't quite sadness and wasn't quite relief — something that lived in the space between the two.
Thank you. That's everything, Maya. That's exactly everything. 💙
Just be careful with yourself. Not careful like — don't live carefully. Just careful with yourself. You matter past the story. Past what happens. Past all of it. Okay? You matter.
I love you so much it makes me crazy sometimes. 😭💙
I love you too. More than you know.
Goodnight, Cass.
Goodnight, Maya.

She set the phone down and stayed sitting in the dark for a while. Not crying — though she could have. She chose not to, the same way she chose a lot of things. She breathed instead. Long and even. The way her body knew how.
She looked at her hand. The faint blue paint still there between her fingers if the light was right. Proof of an afternoon. Proof of something real.
She thought about Ethan's apartment. His sketchbook. The way he had said take all the time you need, like time was something he had plenty of, and would share.
I know it isn't. But I'm not going to tell him that yet.
She lay back down. She looked at the ceiling. Outside, the town was quiet.
Okay.
She closed her eyes.
